passages.title: How Small Habits Become Automatic
passages.meta: Band 6.5 · Learning Psychology · Heading matching fixture

paras.label: A
paras.text: At the beginning, a new habit often feels awkward because every step requires conscious attention. People may forget the action or perform it inconsistently.

paras.label: B
paras.text: After several weeks, the same action can become attached to a stable cue, such as finishing breakfast or arriving at a desk.

paras.label: C
paras.text: Researchers warn that rewards should be meaningful but modest, because excessive rewards may distract learners from the value of the habit itself.

paras.label: D
paras.text: The strongest habits are flexible rather than rigid: people can adapt them when travel, illness or work pressure interrupts the normal routine.

headings: The uncomfortable first stage of a habit
Linking an action to a repeated cue
Why large rewards can become a problem
Adapting a routine when life changes
A historical comparison with classroom learning

blocks.label: A
blocks.reveal: i — Paragraph A describes the awkward first stage when every step requires attention.

blocks.label: B
blocks.reveal: ii — Paragraph B explains how an action becomes attached to a stable cue.

blocks.label: C
blocks.reveal: iii — Paragraph C warns that excessive rewards can distract learners.

blocks.label: D
blocks.reveal: iv — Paragraph D focuses on adapting routines when circumstances change.
